Skip to content
acceleration documentation
acceleration documentation

Contents:

  • API Reference
Back to top
View this page

API Reference¶

High-level façade for the acceleration-kinematics package.

This class mirrors the inverse module’s app: it centralizes file I/O, preset builders, forward/inverse acceleration, and small convenience APIs (classic acceleration, Euler/Quaternion, mixed derivatives) behind a small, testable surface. Importantly, it does not import acceleration.apis to avoid accidental circular imports with any CLI or service layers. It uses the primitives from acceleration.core and the lightweight numpy backend so tests run with zero heavy deps.

Supported operations (extensible): - forward_accel: ẍ = J q̈ + J̇ q̇ (9.283) - inverse_accel: q̈ = J⁺ (ẍ − J̇ q̇) (9.291 / 9.327 damped) - classic: α×r + ω×(ω×r) (tangential + centripetal) - euler_alpha: ZYX Euler angular acceleration (9.127–9.131) - quaternion_SB: quaternion-based acceleration transform (9.175–9.181) - mixed: representative mixed acceleration cases (9.400–9.426)

Problem runner¶

solve(problem_dict) accepts a uniform payload and routes to the requested operation. See docstrings for each method for the expected shape.

Class diagram helpers¶

class_diagram_dot() returns Graphviz DOT, and generate_class_diagram() writes a Mermaid markdown diagram.

class acceleration.app.AccelApp(in_dir: str | Path | None = None, out_dir: str | Path | None = None)[source]¶

High-level application façade for acceleration-kinematics workflows.

Responsibilities¶

  • Build robot backends (Planar 2R out of the box; backends pluggable).

  • Forward & inverse acceleration for chains (9.283 / 9.291 / 9.327).

  • Classic tangential+centripetal term (α×r + ω×(ω×r)).

  • Euler/Quaternion helpers (orientation kinematics).

  • Mixed accelerations (representative helpers for 9.400–9.426).

  • Problem-API utilities used by CLI/tests (solve / batch / from-file).

  • Class diagram export (DOT text) and Mermaid markdown emitter.

class_diagram_dot() → str[source]¶

Return a Graphviz DOT class diagram covering the acceleration package. Uses optional acceleration.tools.diagram.render_dot if available.

classic(alpha: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, omega: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, r: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Return α×r + ω×(ω×r) (tangential + centripetal).

euler_alpha_zyx(angles: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, rates: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, accels: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

ZYX Euler angular acceleration (9.127–9.131 analogue).

forward_accel(chain: ChainKinematics, q: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, qd: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, qdd: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray)[source]¶

Forward acceleration (9.283): ẍ = J q̈ + J̇ q̇

Returns:

result

Return type:

object with .xdd (np.ndarray) for convenience in tests.

generate_class_diagram(out_markdown: Path) → Path[source]¶

Discover classes in the acceleration package and emit a Markdown file with a Mermaid classDiagram block. We include classes, inheritance, and basic associations inferred from type annotations.

inverse_accel(chain: ChainKinematics, q: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, qd: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, xdd: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, *, damping: float = 1e-08)[source]¶

Inverse acceleration (9.291 / 9.327 damped): q̈ = J⁺ (ẍ − J̇ q̇)

Parameters:

damping (DLS damping (λ) for numerical robustness.)

mixed_G_of_B(R: ndarray, omega: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, alpha: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, r: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, vB: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray) → Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]¶

Representative mixed acceleration helper (subset of 9.400–9.426).

Returns:

(a_BG, a_GB)

Return type:

B-expression of G-accel, and G-expression of B-accel.

preset_planar_2r(l1: int | float, l2: int | float) → ChainKinematics[source]¶

Return a ChainKinematics façade over the numpy Planar2R backend.

This mirrors the inverse app’s preset_planar_2r, but here we return a ChainKinematics so we can call forward_accel / inverse_accel.

quaternion_SB(q: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, qd: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray, qdd: Sequence[int | float] | ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Quaternion acceleration transform S_B (9.175–9.181).

solve(problem: Mapping[str, Any]) → Any[source]¶

Solve a single problem dict of the form:

{“op”: “forward|inverse|classic|euler_alpha|quat_SB|mixed”,

“model”: {…}, # only for forward|inverse “payload”: {…}} # op-specific arguments

Returns a JSON-serializable result (lists).

solve_batch(problems: Sequence[Mapping[str, Any]]) → List[Any][source]¶

Solve a batch of heterogeneous problems (results in given order).

solve_from_path(path: str | Path) → Any[source]¶

Load a single problem JSON file and solve it.

class acceleration.app.AppInfo(name: str = 'acceleration-kinematics', version: str = '0.1.0', homepage: str = 'https://example.local/acceleration')[source]¶

Immutable metadata about the application.

homepage: str = 'https://example.local/acceleration'¶
name: str = 'acceleration-kinematics'¶
version: str = '0.1.0'¶

HTTP-and-Python APIs for the acceleration-kinematics package.

Two entry points:

  1. AccelService — a lightweight, framework-free wrapper around AccelApp used by unit tests and Python callers.

  2. create_rest_app() — optional FastAPI factory (only if FastAPI/Pydantic are installed). Not required for unit tests.

Design goals¶

  • Keep imports minimal so tests run without web dependencies.

  • Return plain lists / ndarrays where helpful, matching test expectations.

  • Provide schema-lite validation for each supported operation.

class acceleration.apis.AccelService(app: ~acceleration.app.AccelApp = <acceleration.app.AccelApp object>)[source]¶

Testable Python facade around acceleration.app.AccelApp.

The service keeps a tiny surface that maps 1:1 to unit-testable actions: forward acceleration, inverse acceleration, classic α×r + ω×(ω×r), Euler/Quaternion helpers, and mixed-acceleration utilities.

app: AccelApp = <acceleration.app.AccelApp object>¶
class_diagram_mermaid() → str[source]¶
classic(alpha: Sequence[float], omega: Sequence[float], r: Sequence[float]) → ndarray[source]¶
euler_alpha(angles: Sequence[float], rates: Sequence[float], accels: Sequence[float]) → ndarray[source]¶
forward(l1: float, l2: float, q: Sequence[float], qd: Sequence[float], qdd: Sequence[float]) → ndarray[source]¶
inverse(l1: float, l2: float, q: Sequence[float], qd: Sequence[float], xdd: Sequence[float], *, damping: float = 1e-08) → ndarray[source]¶
mixed(R: Sequence[Sequence[float]], omega: Sequence[float], alpha: Sequence[float], r: Sequence[float], vB: Sequence[float]) → Dict[str, List[float]][source]¶
problem_from_file(path: str) → Dict[str, Any][source]¶

Load a problem JSON file (generic acceleration payload).

quat_sb(q: Sequence[float], qd: Sequence[float], qdd: Sequence[float]) → ndarray[source]¶
save_result(out_path_or_name: str, result: Any) → str[source]¶

Save a generic result JSON and return the actual file path (as string).

solve(problem: Dict[str, Any]) → Any[source]¶
solve_batch(problems: Iterable[Dict[str, Any]]) → List[Any][source]¶
static validate_problem(problem: Dict[str, Any]) → Tuple[bool, str | None][source]¶
Validate a generic acceleration problem dict of the form:

{“op”: “…”, “model”: {…}, “payload”: {…}}

Supported ops:

forward, inverse, classic, euler_alpha, quat_sb, mixed

Configuration for the model, should be a dictionary conforming to [ConfigDict][pydantic.config.ConfigDict].

r: List[float]¶

Configuration for the model, should be a dictionary conforming to [ConfigDict][pydantic.config.ConfigDict].

l2: float¶

Configuration for the model, should be a dictionary conforming to [ConfigDict][pydantic.config.ConfigDict].

qd: List[float]¶

qd: List[float]¶

Configuration for the model, should be a dictionary conforming to [ConfigDict][pydantic.config.ConfigDict].

class acceleration.apis.ProblemModel(*, op: str, payload: Dict[str, Any], model: Dict[str, Any] | None = None)[source]¶

Generic acceleration problem: {‘op’: str, ‘payload’: {…}, ‘model’?: {…}}

Configuration for the model, should be a dictionary conforming to [ConfigDict][pydantic.config.ConfigDict].

op: str¶
payload: Dict[str, Any]¶
class acceleration.apis.QuatSBRequest(*, q: List[float], qd: List[float], qdd: List[float])[source]¶
model_config: ClassVar[ConfigDict] = {}¶

Configuration for the model, should be a dictionary conforming to [ConfigDict][pydantic.config.ConfigDict].

q: List[float]¶
qd: List[float]¶

acceleration.apis.create_rest_app()[source]¶

Factory that returns a FastAPI app exposing the service as HTTP endpoints.

Return type:

fastapi.FastAPI

Raises:

ImportError – If FastAPI or Pydantic are not installed.

Core math, object model, and acceleration-kinematics façades.

What’s inside¶

  • Rotation, Transform — SO(3)/SE(3) helpers (self-contained)

  • ClassicAccel — α×r + ω×(ω×r) (tangential + centripetal)

  • AccelTransforms — S = α̃ + ω̃²; omega/alpha from Ṙ,R̈

  • MixedAcceleration — representative mixed-derivative helpers

  • EulerKinematics (ZYX) — α from Euler angles (9.127–9.131)

  • QuaternionKinematics — quaternion-based acceleration S_B

  • ChainKinematics — forward/inverse acceleration via backends

Design notes¶

  • This module is framework-free and focuses on pure math + façades.

  • Backends (NumPy 2R, Pinocchio, Drake) live in acceleration.backends.* and are consumed through a tiny Backend interface.

  • All public methods are small and deterministic — easy to test with pytest.

class acceleration.core.AccelTransforms[source]¶

Small helpers for rotation derivatives and the acceleration transform:

S = α̃ + ω̃² (appears throughout §9)

static S_from(alpha: ndarray, omega: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Return S = α̃ + ω̃² given α, ω.

static alpha_from_Rddot(R: ndarray, Rdot: ndarray, Rddot: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

α from R̈ Rᵀ = α̃ + ω̃² (9.31–9.36 style).

static omega_from_Rdot(R: ndarray, Rdot: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

ω from Ṙ Rᵀ = ω̃ (9.11–9.14 style identity).

class acceleration.core.ChainKinematics(backend: Backend, frame: str = 'ee')[source]¶

Thin façade over a Backend that provides Jacobian-based forward/inverse acceleration in a frame (e.g., an end-effector).

Forward acceleration (9.283):

ẍ = J(q) q̈ + J̇(q, q̇) q̇

Inverse acceleration (9.291 / 9.327):

q̈ = J⁺ (ẍ − J̇ q̇) (DLS when damp > 0)

backend: Backend¶
forward_accel(q: Sequence[float], qd: Sequence[float], qdd: Sequence[float]) → ndarray[source]¶
frame: str = 'ee'¶
inverse_accel(q: Sequence[float], qd: Sequence[float], xdd: Sequence[float], *, damp: float = 1e-08) → ndarray[source]¶
class acceleration.core.ClassicAccel[source]¶

Classic rigid-body acceleration: a = α×r + ω×(ω×r).

static at_point(alpha: ndarray, omega: ndarray, r: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶
class acceleration.core.EulerKinematics(seq: str = 'ZYX')[source]¶

Euler-angle angular acceleration. This minimal drop supports ZYX, which covers the common robotics convention and maps to (9.127–9.131).

alpha(angles: Sequence[float], rates: Sequence[float], accels: Sequence[float]) → ndarray[source]¶

Return angular acceleration α for ZYX Euler using a numerically-stable Jacobian-time-derivative approach (sufficient for unit tests).

seq: str = 'ZYX'¶
class acceleration.core.MixedAcceleration[source]¶

Representative mixed-derivative helper from §9.4xx family: returns (^B G a, ^G B a) for a point with local velocity v_B.

static G_of_B(R: ndarray, omega: ndarray, alpha: ndarray, r: ndarray, vB: ndarray) → tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]¶
class acceleration.core.QuaternionKinematics[source]¶

Quaternion-based acceleration mapping for S_B; minimal, test-friendly. q = [w, x, y, z], q̇, q̈.

S_B(q: ndarray, qd: ndarray, qdd: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Return a vector consistent with S_B action using finite differentiation of ω(q, q̇). For unit tests and numeric workflows this is sufficient.

static quat_omega(q: ndarray, qd: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Angular velocity ω from quaternion rate (compact linear map).

class acceleration.core.Rotation[source]¶

Minimal SO(3) utilities used by acceleration kinematics.

static Rx(theta: float) → ndarray[source]¶
static Ry(theta: float) → ndarray[source]¶
static Rz(theta: float) → ndarray[source]¶
static axis_angle(R: ndarray) → Tuple[float, ndarray][source]¶

Return (phi, u) such that R = exp(phi * [u]^).

class acceleration.core.Transform(R: ndarray, t: ndarray)[source]¶

SE(3) transform (R, t) with thin utilities.

R: ndarray¶
apply(p: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶
as_matrix() → ndarray[source]¶
static eye() → Transform[source]¶
static from_Rt(R: ndarray, t: ndarray) → Transform[source]¶
static from_matrix(T: ndarray) → Transform[source]¶
inv() → Transform[source]¶
t: ndarray¶

I/O, schema, and builders for acceleration-kinematics problems.

Problem shape¶

Acceleration problems are expressed as a small envelope:

{

“op”: “forward” | “inverse” | “classic” | “euler_alpha” | “quat_sb” | “mixed”, “payload”: { … op-specific fields … }, “model”: { “kind”: “planar2r”, “l1”: <float>, “l2”: <float> } # required for forward/inverse

}

Notes

  • Unlike the inverse module, acceleration workflows do not require low-level DH/MDH chain specs here. We build ChainKinematics façades from high-level models.

  • YAML input is supported if PyYAML is installed.

  • Validation uses jsonschema if available; otherwise we raise clear errors.

Examples

Forward acceleration (planar 2R): {

“op”: “forward”, “model”: {“kind”: “planar2r”, “l1”: 1.0, “l2”: 0.7}, “payload”: {“q”: [0.1, 0.2], “qd”: [0.3, -0.1], “qdd”: [0.0, 1.2]}

}

Inverse acceleration: {

“op”: “inverse”, “model”: {“kind”: “planar2r”, “l1”: 1.0, “l2”: 0.7}, “payload”: {“q”: [0.1, 0.2], “qd”: [0.3, -0.1], “xdd”: [0.2, -0.5]}

}

Classic point acceleration: {

“op”: “classic”, “payload”: {“alpha”: [0,0,1], “omega”: [0,0,3], “r”: [0.2,0.0,0.0]}

}

Mixed (representative §9.4xx helper): {

“op”: “mixed”, “payload”: {

“R”: [[1,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,1]], “omega”: [0,0,2], “alpha”: [0,0,1], “r”: [0.1,0.0,0.0], “vB”: [0.0,0.05,0.0]

}

}

acceleration.io.build_chain_from_model(model: Mapping[str, Any], *, validate: bool = False) → ChainKinematics[source]¶

Build a ChainKinematics from a high-level model dict. Currently supported kinds:

  • {“kind”: “planar2r”, “l1”: …, “l2”: …}

acceleration.io.chain_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶

Minimal schema for a chain descriptor (what build_chain_from_model returns). Tests only check presence of the top-level “type” key; we still provide a sensible structure with n (DOF) and an optional links array.

acceleration.io.load_json_or_yaml(path: str | Path) → Dict[str, Any][source]¶

Load dict from a JSON or YAML file based on extension.

acceleration.io.load_problem_from_file(path: str | Path, *, validate: bool = True) → Dict[str, Any][source]¶

Load an acceleration problem from JSON/YAML file.

acceleration.io.model_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶

Schema for high-level model specs used by acceleration presets.

acceleration.io.payload_classic_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶
acceleration.io.payload_euler_alpha_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶
acceleration.io.payload_forward_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶
acceleration.io.payload_inverse_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶
acceleration.io.payload_mixed_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶
acceleration.io.payload_quat_sb_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶
acceleration.io.problem_schema() → Dict[str, Any][source]¶

Top-level acceleration problem schema.

Uses if/then to require model for forward/inverse ops, while keeping it optional for other ops.

acceleration.io.save_problem_json(path: str | Path, problem: Mapping[str, Any]) → None[source]¶

Save a problem dict as pretty JSON (without validation).

acceleration.io.save_result_json(path: str | Path, result: Any) → None[source]¶

Save a generic result as pretty JSON.

acceleration.io.validate_high_level_model(model: Mapping[str, Any]) → None[source]¶

Compatibility alias for tests expecting this name.

acceleration.io.validate_model(model: Mapping[str, Any]) → None[source]¶

Validate a high-level acceleration model against model_schema().

acceleration.io.validate_problem(problem: Mapping[str, Any]) → None[source]¶

Validate an acceleration problem against problem_schema().

Utility decorators and math helpers for acceleration kinematics. […docstring unchanged…]

acceleration.utils.S_from(alpha: Sequence[float] | ndarray, omega: Sequence[float] | ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Return the acceleration transform matrix S = α̃ + ω̃².

acceleration.utils.TOL: float = 1e-09¶

Default absolute tolerance for equality checks.

acceleration.utils.adjoint(T: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Adjoint representation Ad_T of an SE(3) transform T.

Returns:

[[R, 0],

[p^ R, R]]

Return type:

(6,6) ndarray

acceleration.utils.almost_equal(a: ndarray, b: ndarray, *, atol: float = 1e-09, rtol: float = 1e-09) → bool[source]¶

Elementwise closeness for arrays.

acceleration.utils.alpha_from_Rddot(R: ndarray, Rdot: ndarray, Rddot: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

BODY-FRAME angular acceleration from the kinematic identities:

Rᵀ Ṙ = ω^ Rᵀ R̈ = α^ + ω^ ω^

Numerically robust computation in extended precision:

α^ = skew( Rᵀ R̈ - ω^ ω^ )

acceleration.utils.asvec(x: Iterable[float] | ndarray, n: int) → ndarray[source]¶

Strict 1-D vector coercion with length check (great for unit tests).

Preserves complex dtype when x is complex. This is important for complex-step differentiation paths that temporarily promote to complex.

acceleration.utils.clamp(x: float, lo: float, hi: float) → float[source]¶

Clamp a scalar to [lo, hi].

acceleration.utils.classic_accel(alpha: Sequence[float], omega: Sequence[float], r: Sequence[float]) → ndarray[source]¶
Classic rigid-body point acceleration (body frame):

a = α×r + ω×(ω×r)

Compute using the double cross in extended precision to reduce cancellation relative to forming W @ (W @ r). Return float64.

acceleration.utils.ensure_shape(*shape: int) → Callable[[Callable[[...], ndarray]], Callable[[...], ndarray]][source]¶

Decorator that validates the ndarray shape of the returned value.

acceleration.utils.homogeneous(R: ndarray, t: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Compose a 4×4 homogeneous transform from (R, t).

acceleration.utils.jdot_qdot_fd(J_fn: Callable[[ndarray], ndarray], q: Sequence[float], qd: Sequence[float], eps: float = 1e-06) → ndarray[source]¶

Compute (J̇(q, q̇)) q̇ via a high-accuracy 5-point central stencil along q̇:

d/dt J(q(t))|_{t=0} with q(t) = q + t q̇ ≈ [-J(q+2h q̇) + 8 J(q+h q̇) - 8 J(q-h q̇) + J(q-2h q̇)] / (12 h)

Then multiply by q̇ on the right.

acceleration.utils.normalize(v: ndarray, eps: float = 1e-12) → ndarray[source]¶

Return a unit-length copy of vector v; if ||v|| < eps, returns v unchanged.

acceleration.utils.omega_from_Rdot(R: ndarray, Rdot: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

BODY-FRAME angular velocity from the kinematic identity:

Rᵀ Ṙ = [ω]^ (ω expressed in the body frame)

Compute in extended precision for robustness, then return float64.

acceleration.utils.skew(v: Sequence[float] | ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Skew-symmetric matrix [v]^ of a 3-vector.

Return type:

(3,3) ndarray with dtype preserved from v (supports complex-step).

acceleration.utils.timed(fn: Callable[[...], T]) → Callable[[...], T][source]¶

Decorator that measures wall-clock runtime of fn.

The wrapper exposes a float attribute last_runtime with the duration.

acceleration.utils.vex(S: ndarray) → ndarray[source]¶

Vee operator (inverse of skew). Extracts the vector from a 3×3 skew-symmetric matrix.

Preserves dtype (supports complex-step).
